era_context: The year 922 CE falls within the early 10th century, a period of significant political fragmentation across Eurasia. The Abbasid Caliphate's authority was declining; regional Islamic dynasties were consolidating. In Europe, feudalism was solidifying and the Holy Roman Empire was emerging. In East Asia, the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period was ongoing, with the Later Liang Dynasty still ruling northern China. In the Islamic world, theological and political schism deepened. This was an era of transition and regional consolidation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (no written records)

events.title: Later Liang Dynasty in decline; Later Tang rising
events.description: In 922, the Later Liang Dynasty (907–923) was in its final year of rule in northern China. The dynasty, founded by Zhu Wen (852–912), had been declining under his successors. By 922, the Later Liang was facing military pressure from rival warlords, particularly Li Cunxu of the Shatuo Turks, who would establish the Later Tang Dynasty in 923. The year 922 marked the eve of a major dynastic transition in northern China.
key_figures: Zhu Wen (founder of Later Liang, deceased)
Li Cunxu (Shatuo warlord, future founder of Later Tang)
events.certainty_note: The Later Liang Dynasty's dates (907–923) are well-established. Specific events within 922 are not precisely documented; this reflects the broader decline process.

events.title: Henry I (the Fowler) consolidates East Frankish power
events.description: In 922, Henry I (r. 919–936), known as 'the Fowler,' was consolidating his rule over the East Frankish Kingdom. He had become king in 919 and by 922 was establishing himself as a strong ruler through military campaigns and internal consolidation. Henry I's reign marked the transition from the weak Carolingian successor states to a more centralized Germanic kingdom.
events.certainty_note: Henry I's reign dates (919–936) are well-established. Specific events within 922 are not precisely documented.

events.title: Goryeo consolidation under Wang Geon
events.description: In 922, Wang Geon (877–943) was consolidating the Goryeo Kingdom in Korea. Goryeo had been established in 918 and by 922 was in its fifth year of existence. Wang Geon was absorbing remaining Later Three Kingdoms territories and establishing administrative structures. This period saw the cultural and political foundation of medieval Korea.
key_figures: Wang Geon (founder and king of Goryeo)
events.certainty_note: Wang Geon's reign dates (918–943) are well-established. Specific events within 922 are not precisely documented.

events.title: Khitan Liao consolidation under Abaoji
events.description: In 922, the Khitan Liao state under Abaoji (r. 907–926) was consolidating its power in Manchuria and Mongolia. Abaoji had declared himself emperor in 916 and was centralizing Khitan confederation under imperial rule. By 922, Liao was becoming a major regional power. This period saw the development of Liao's administrative and military structures.
events.certainty_note: Abaoji's reign dates (907–926) are well-established. Specific events within 922 are not precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The historical record for 922 is sparse; most events are inferred from broader reign contexts rather than specific annual documentation.
historiographic_note: The year 922 is sparsely documented. Most information comes from later compilations (11th–13th centuries) that provide reign-level rather than year-level detail. Specific events within 922 are rarely documented in contemporary sources. The record is dominated by political and dynastic history; economic, cultural, and social developments are largely absent. Non-literate regions are entirely absent from documentation.

graph_edges.note: Later Liang's decline in 922 led to Later Tang's establishment in 923
